item.title: The Blessed One replied to Piṅgiya: “Seeing the danger in all forms, Beings living heedlessly, Afflicted by forms — Therefore, Piṅgiya, do not be heedless.…
item.description:
The Blessed One replied to Piṅgiya:
“Seeing the danger in all forms,
Beings living heedlessly,
Afflicted by forms —
Therefore, Piṅgiya, do not be heedless.
Abandon forms so as not to come to birth again.

Seeing beings, human folk,
Falling into craving,
Burned by old age,
Overcome by old age —
Therefore, Piṅgiya, do not be heedless.
Abandon craving so as not to come to birth again.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.17
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. This, monks, is not called the ocean…
item.description:
“Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. This, monks, is not called the ocean in the noble one’s discipline. Here, this world with its devas, Māras, and Brahmās, with its ascetics and brahmins, with its gods and humans, for the most part is submerged in them, tangled like a ball of thread, covered like a tangled skein, become like a tangled grass rope, unable to pass beyond the plane of misery, the bad destination, the lower realm, saṃsāra. Monks, there are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … Monks, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. This, monks, is not called the ocean in the noble one’s discipline. Here, this world … for the most part is submerged in them … unable to pass beyond the plane of misery, the bad destination, the lower realm, saṃsāra.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.229
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “What do you think, Māgandiya? Here, a person formerly was infatuated with forms cognizable by the eye that were desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with…
item.description:
“What do you think, Māgandiya? Here, a person formerly was infatuated with forms cognizable by the eye that were desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. After some time, having known as they actually are the origination, the passing away, the gratification, the peril, and the escape in the case of forms, he abandons craving for forms, removes the fever for forms, and dwells with craving cut off, with mind inwardly peaceful.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 75 – Māgandiyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are agreeable and disagreeable. Though they touch the mind again and again, they do not overpower…
item.description:
“Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are agreeable and disagreeable. Though they touch the mind again and again, they do not overpower the mind and remain. Because the mind is not overpowered, energy and exertion arise, not laziness; mindfulness is established, not lost; the body becomes tranquil, not feverish; the mind becomes concentrated and one-pointed. Seeing this fruit of heedfulness, monks, regarding these monks, I declare that one should strive heedfully with regard to the six sense bases. Monks, there are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … Monks, there are phenomena cognizable by the mind that are agreeable and disagreeable. Though they touch the mind again and again, they do not overpower the mind and remain. Because the mind is not overpowered, energy and exertion arise, not laziness; mindfulness is established, not lost; the body becomes tranquil, not feverish; the mind becomes concentrated and one-pointed. Seeing this fruit of heedfulness, monks, regarding these monks, I declare that one should strive heedfully with regard to the six sense bases.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.134
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Householder, having known that sensual desire is a defilement of the mind, that noble disciple abandons sensual desire, the defilement of the mind; having known…
item.description:
“Householder, having known that sensual desire is a defilement of the mind, that noble disciple abandons sensual desire, the defilement of the mind; having known that ill-will is a defilement of the mind, he abandons ill-will; having known that sloth and torpor are defilements of the mind, he abandons sloth and torpor; having known that restlessness and remorse are defilements of the mind, he abandons restlessness and remorse; having known that doubt is a defilement of the mind, he abandons doubt. This, householder, is called a noble disciple of great wisdom, of broad wisdom, who has entered the sphere of clear vision, endowed with wisdom. This is called being endowed with wisdom.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 4.61
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Thus he abides contemplating the body as a body internally; or he abides contemplating the body as a body externally; or he abides contemplating the…
item.description:
Thus he abides contemplating the body as a body internally; or he abides contemplating the body as a body externally; or he abides contemplating the body as a body both internally and externally. Or he abides contemplating the nature of arising in the body; or he abides contemplating the nature of passing away in the body; or he abides contemplating the nature of both arising and passing away in the body. ‘There is a body,’ he establishes mindfulness just enough for knowledge and mindfulness. And he abides independent, not clinging to anything in the world. Thus, monks, a monk abides contemplating the body as a body.”

From: Dīgha Nikāya 22 – Mahāsatipaṭṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘My mind will be internally established, well fixed. Evil, unwholesome states that arise will not invade my mind and will not stand.’ Thus, monk, you…
item.description:
“‘My mind will be internally established, well fixed. Evil, unwholesome states that arise will not invade my mind and will not stand.’ Thus, monk, you should train.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 8.63
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, not knowing and not seeing the eye as it actually is; not knowing and not seeing forms … eye-consciousness … eye-contact as it actually…
item.description:
“Monks, not knowing and not seeing the eye as it actually is; not knowing and not seeing forms … eye-consciousness … eye-contact as it actually is; not knowing and not seeing the feeling—whether pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—that arises dependent on eye-contact as it actually is, then he becomes attached to the eye, to forms, to eye-consciousness, to eye-contact. Dependent on eye-contact, the feeling—pleasant, painful, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—arises; he becomes attached to that feeling. When he dwells contemplating gratification, attached, tied, infatuated, the five aggregates of clinging accumulate for the future. And his craving—which leads to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting here and there—increases. His bodily torments increase, his mental torments increase, his bodily fevers increase, his mental fevers increase, his bodily pains increase, his mental pains increase. He experiences bodily suffering and mental suffering.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 149 – Mahāsaḷāyatanikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “One who finds no core among becomings, Like one picking flowers from a fig tree — That monk abandons the near and far shore, Like…
item.description:
“One who finds no core among becomings,
Like one picking flowers from a fig tree —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

“One in whom there is no anger inside,
Who has gone beyond existence and non-existence —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

“One whose thoughts have been dispersed,
Internally well-ordered, without remainder —
That monk abandons the near and far shore,
Like a serpent sheds its worn-out skin.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 1.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, monks, is dwelling heedfully? If one lives with restraint of the eye faculty, monks, his mind is not defiled by forms cognizable by…
item.description:
“And how, monks, is dwelling heedfully? If one lives with restraint of the eye faculty, monks, his mind is not defiled by forms cognizable by the eye. For one with an undefiled mind, gladness arises. With gladness, rapture arises. With rapture, the body becomes tranquil. With a tranquil body, he experiences ease. For one at ease, the mind becomes concentrated. When the mind is concentrated, phenomena are clearly seen. When phenomena are clearly seen, he is called ‘one who dwells heedfully’ … the ear faculty … the nose faculty … the tongue faculty … the body faculty … If one lives with restraint of the mind faculty, his mind is not defiled by phenomena cognizable by the mind. For one with an undefiled mind, gladness arises … he is called ‘one who dwells heedfully.’ Thus, monks, is dwelling heedfully.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.97
📖 Read this sutta
